All reports
Fenn & Hart
PITCHPRO

Competitive Intelligence Report

The battlefield map: where PitchPro wins, where it is exposed, and the open lane to march to.

Prepared by Fenn & Hart
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Roofr is the one direct threat, a Series B all-in-one roofing platform with 12,000+ roofers and a structural supplier moat through ABC Supply. PitchPro cannot win on breadth or price against it. The winnable ground is the operational backbone the funded incumbents fail at, clean two-way QuickBooks and a real mobile experience for the $2-10M roofer, paired with predictable flat-rate cost. The single largest exposure is self-inflicted: PitchPro’s own site claims to be the “only all-in-one” (false on Roofr’s own pages) and ships a broken demo and a checkout stuck in test mode.

PitchPro competes against four named companies and one harder market reality. Of the four, only one is a head-to-head rival.

Roofr is the real contest. Its homepage meta calls it “All-in-one roofing software with CRM, instant estimates, proposals, measurements, payments & material ordering”1, it carries 12,000+ roofing companies2, it took a TCV-led Series B with ABC Supply participating3, and it prices flat with a permanent $0 tier4. PitchPro’s founding belief that no all-in-one rival exists is falsified on Roofr’s own live pages1.

Housecall Pro is a horizontal field-service platform across 50+ trades, not a roofing product. Its roofing landing page carries no aerial measurement, no insurance supplement workflow, and no storm-damage tracking5, and roofing does not even appear in its own plan-recommendation quiz6. CompanyCam is a photo-documentation tool with no native CRM, estimating, scheduling, or invoicing7; its Beam acquisition is early-stage and the two companies “continue to operate independently”8. EagleView is a measurement and imagery company holding a genuine cornered resource, 300+ enforced patents and a 25-year aerial library9, but it sells no business-management software and is a data input PitchPro can consume.

Where PitchPro wins: the operational backbone. Roofr has no native mobile app, only a browser experience with a PWA on its roadmap10, and its QuickBooks integration is Beta and one-directional11. Those are the exact failures roofers complain about across incumbents12. Where PitchPro ties: consolidation and flat pricing, both of which Roofr already offers. Where PitchPro lags: proof. It is pre-revenue, its demo is a placeholder, and its checkout sits in Stripe test mode, so the “runs itself” promise cannot be shown today.

The single biggest open lane is predictable total cost of ownership for the active $2-10M roofer. Roofr’s $13 per measurement report stacks on top of subscription, pushing an active 20-report-per-month roofer with two common add-ons to roughly $519 per month before a single report beyond that13. PitchPro’s flat $399 for up to 20 people wins on a predictable bill for that buyer, though a low-volume roofer can still come in under $399 on Roofr, so the claim is predictability and never cheapest13.

1. Competitor Matrix and Client Snapshot

The dimensions below are the ones a $2-10M roofing contractor weighs at purchase: whether the tool is built for roofing, whether it covers the full lead-to-cash workflow, how it charges, and whether the money plumbing (QuickBooks, payments) actually holds.

Capability comparison - PitchPro vs. named competitors Figure 1. PitchPro matches roofing-native scope, while Roofr’s gaps are mobile and accounting. Capability presence per competitor; PitchPro’s pre-revenue items are marked unproven. Use on sales calls when prospects ask “how do you compare to X?”

Dimension Roofr Housecall Pro CompanyCam EagleView
What it is All-in-one roofing platform1 Horizontal field-service software, 50+ trades14 Photo-documentation app7 Aerial measurement and imagery9
Roofing-native Yes, built for roofers15 No; generic roofing landing page only5 Vertical landing page, not native architecture16 Roofing is one of several verticals9
CRM / pipeline Yes17 Yes (horizontal) No7 No9
Estimating / proposals Yes, e-signature17 Estimates yes; proposal tool is a paid add-on18 No (Beam not integrated)8 Measurement data only9
Measurement Core, $13/report17 None native5 None Core, $15-$38/report19
Invoicing / payments Yes (CC 2.8%+$0.30, ACH 0.5%)17 Yes No (pre-Beam)8 No9
Material ordering ABC Supply, SRS, QXO20 No No No
Mobile Browser only; PWA on roadmap10 Native iOS + Android14 Native, photo-first7 Web/report delivery
QuickBooks Beta, one-way, Scale only11 Essentials+ only21 No native No
Pricing model Flat, no per-seat, $0 tier4 Per-plan with seat caps + add-ons22 Per-user, 3-seat minimum23 Per-report, sales-gated platform24
Funding Series B, TCV + ABC Supply3 $125M Series D (June 2022)25 Series C, $2B valuation26 PE-owned (Vista + Clearlake)27
Review posture Capterra 4.6/101; G2 4.9/4028 G2 4.3/204 vs. own 4.7 claim29 G2 4.7/32; Capterra 4.630 Capterra 4.3/4431
  1. PitchPro snapshot. PitchPro is a pre-revenue, roofing-native all-in-one platform aimed at the $2-10M residential roofing contractor, the underserved band between basic general tools and premium enterprise suites32. Its pricing posture is flat: $399 per month for up to 20 people, unlimited use, no per-report fee, no per-seat tax13.
  2. Claimed differentiators. PitchPro’s site currently leads with “the only all-in-one” roofing platform and a runs-itself automation story (an auto-advancing job whiteboard and a 15-second AI quote). The all-in-one superlative is false against Roofr and four of the top six roofing vendors who use the same phrase133.
  3. Visible strengths. A roofing-native scope spanning lead to QuickBooks, flat predictable pricing, and an automation concept aimed squarely at the operational gaps incumbents leave open12.
  4. Visible weaknesses. No customers and no proof; a placeholder demo; a checkout in Stripe test mode; an empty blog and an empty about page. The runs-itself half of the pitch cannot be demonstrated live today.

2. Per-Competitor Deep Dives

2.1 Roofr, the funded all-in-one that already holds the headline

URL. https://roofr.com

Verbatim positioning. The homepage meta description reads “All-in-one roofing software with CRM, instant estimates, proposals, measurements, payments & material ordering. Rated 4.7/5 stars. Start free, no credit card.”1 The homepage video is titled “Roofr: The all in one software for roofers,” the Series B press release calls it “the all-in-one roofing CRM designed for growing roofing businesses,” and the small-business landing page repeats the same phrase, five distinct live locations carrying the identical claim1.

  • The 7 Powers, real against claimed. Roofr’s strongest real power is a partial cornered resource through ABC Supply. ABC Supply, the largest US roofing materials distributor, is simultaneously a Series B equity investor and a featured material-ordering integration partner inside Roofr3. A second supply-chain integration with SRS Distribution went live on July 7, 2025, putting live pricing and inventory from 430+ SRS locations one click from a Roofr proposal20. That distributor-plus-equity combination is a structural advantage a new entrant cannot assemble off the shelf. Roofr also holds growing switching costs through full workflow capture, lead to measurement to proposal to payment in one platform17. Its claimed process power does not hold: a Capterra owner noted the measurement system relies on satellite and Google imagery rather than proprietary technology34. Scale is partial and building, not dominant, at 12,000+ companies2.
  1. Target and scope. Roofr targets small and growing residential roofing contractors in the US and Canada, and explicitly cedes mid-market and enterprise to AccuLynx and JobNimbus35. It does not charge per seat. Plans run Starter at $0 per month with $19 reports, Essentials at $249 per month with five seats and $13 reports, and Scale at $349 per month with unlimited seats and $13 reports4. Measurement reports are always pay-as-you-go on top of the subscription4.
  2. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “A full suite of tools you need to run your business, with one login.” “Built for roofers, by roofers.” “Our CEO was a roofer and built Roofr to solve real problems.”15 “$13 USD to get a report delivered within 2 hours.” “Rated 4.7/5 stars with over 1,000+ reviews.”15
  3. Strengths. Top-tier venture validation from TCV, the firm behind Netflix, Spotify, and Toast3. A permanent free Starter tier that acquires customers at zero cost and upsells into $249-$349 paid plans36. Named customer outcomes on the homepage, including a CEO who reports closing $2.5M in year one37. Strong review posture, 4.9/5 across 40 G2 reviews and a top G2 Fall 2025 ranking for support, usability, and value28.
  4. Weaknesses. No native mobile app as of June 2026; the platform is browser-based with a PWA on the roadmap and not shipped as of April 202610. QuickBooks integration is labeled Beta, one-directional, and Scale-plan only, with no reconciliation for bookkeepers11. Change Orders are listed “Coming soon” on the live pricing table, and production scheduling is a stated 2026 focus area, meaning it was a gap through 202510. Measurement reliability draws repeated complaints: an owner reported pitches “regularly incorrect” with a 50% price increase after heavier use38, and another switched away after three consecutive reports came back as unavailable imagery39.
  5. Recent dated activity. Series B announced January 28, 2025, TCV-led with ABC Supply, Crosslink Capital, Euclid Ventures, MGFO, and i2BF participating, amount undisclosed3. Pricing repriced February 1, 2025, to $19 reports on free and $13 on paid40. SRS Distribution real-time pricing integration launched July 7, 202520. Pricing tiers restructured in March 2026 to the current Starter/Measure+/Essentials/Scale names41.
  6. Overlap with PitchPro. Direct and broad. Roofr and PitchPro both cover CRM, instant estimates, proposals with e-signature, invoicing, payments, and calendar17. Both price flat. Roofr is the rival PitchPro’s positioning must account for first.
  7. Inline whitespace. Roofr underserves the field and the rural job. With no native app and no offline mode, crews in poor-signal areas open the desktop site on a phone, and satellite imagery gaps force a DIY fallback on a meaningful share of rural roofs1039. Roofr also has no true two-way QuickBooks sync, no labor tracking, and no drip marketing11. A roofing-native platform that ships a real mobile field experience and clean two-way accounting takes the operational backbone Roofr has left unbuilt.

2.2 Housecall Pro, the horizontal platform wearing a roofing label

URL. https://www.housecallpro.com

Verbatim positioning. The sitewide og:site_name reads “Housecall Pro

  • The #1 all-in-one solution for your home services business,” a horizontal claim across 50+ trades14. The roofing landing page hero reads “Built for roofing Pros
  • Win more jobs and get paid faster with scheduling, estimates, and invoicing made for roofing companies.”5
  • The 7 Powers, in plain terms. Housecall Pro’s real strength is horizontal scale and brand recognition, 200,000+ Pros across 50+ trades25, with switching costs from QuickBooks sync, customer history, and price-book lock-in. None of that scale is roofing-specific. The brand is strong in HVAC and plumbing and weak in roofing, and the counter-positioning it claims runs against ServiceTitan and Jobber, not against any roofing-specific rival. There is no cornered resource and no proprietary roofing process here; the AI features are general-purpose add-ons.
  1. Target and scope. Solo operators to mid-size teams of 1-10+ users across HVAC, plumbing, electrical, cleaning, landscaping, and 40+ other trades14. Plans run Basic at $59/mo (1 user), Essentials at $149/mo (up to 5 users), and MAX at $299/mo (up to 8 users base, $35/mo each additional)22. QuickBooks is gated to Essentials and above21.
  2. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “Tools built for your industry.” “Pricing that fits any crew, from solo ops to full teams.” “Join 200K Pros across 50+ trades.” “AI team members to automate your work.”22
  3. Strengths. Real footprint and capital, 200,000+ Pros and a $125M Series D from Permira and Vista Credit Partners25. Broad product surface including scheduling, dispatch, payments, payroll, and a CompanyCam integration for jobsite photos. Named social proof from cleaning and coatings businesses on the pricing page.
  4. Weaknesses. The roofing fit is hollow. The roofing page (published October 17, 2025) carries zero aerial measurement, no insurance supplement workflow, and no storm-damage tracking5, and roofing is absent from the plan-recommendation quiz that lists ten other trades6. The “all-in-one” claim is undercut by the add-on stack: proposal tool, service plans, pipeline, call answering, campaigns, websites, accounting, payroll, and GPS are all separate paid line items42. BBB shows 76 complaints over three years (32 in the last 12 months), the company is not BBB accredited, and the dominant pattern is billing and cancellation friction, including a complaint that the company “attempted to withdraw $189.00 from my bank twice in one try”43. The G2 rating, 4.3/5 across 204 reviews, sits below the 4.7 the footer claims29. On June 15, 2026, founder-CEO Mike Beaudoin was replaced by Stan Chia, whose background is consumer marketplace and ticketing rather than the trades44.
  5. Recent dated activity. $125M Series D closed June 23, 2022, the last known round; no new funding or acquisitions in the four years since25. New CEO named June 15, 202644.
  6. Overlap with PitchPro. Shallow on roofing. Housecall Pro overlaps on generic scheduling, estimates, invoicing, and the “all-in-one” vocabulary, but it ships no roofing-specific workflow, so the head-to-head only happens when a roofer is mid-evaluation between a horizontal tool and a vertical one.
  7. Inline whitespace. Everything roofing-specific is open next to Housecall Pro. It has no EagleView or HOVER integration, no insurance claim or adjuster documentation, and no shingle-by-square material costing545. A roofing platform that embeds measurement into the estimate flow and maps revenue by job type to QuickBooks owns a workflow Housecall Pro cannot match without a vertical rebuild.

2.3 CompanyCam, the photo tool narrating an all-in-one future it has not built

URL. https://companycam.com

Verbatim positioning. CompanyCam self-describes as “the #1 Job Site Tracking App for Contractors.”16 Its roofing page promises to “show a client exactly what’s going on with their project … and prove you didn’t damage anything in the process.”16 The Series C narrative reaches further, naming a vision to help contractors “do good work, win the right customers, and get paid fast”46.

  • The 7 Powers, real against claimed. The one real power is process power in photo capture, a genuinely best-in-class photo UX, plus partial branding as the default photo-documentation tool in roofing730. The scale claim does not hold as a moat: photos do not create cross-customer network value, and switching costs are shallow because photo archives export cleanly7. The aspirational platform identity has no product behind it.
  1. Target and scope. Roofing, siding, gutters, HVAC, general contracting, and 50+ trades, sweet spot 0-49 employees, with a three-user minimum on every plan and no single-user option23. Pricing starts at $79/mo for three users (annual, Pro), $29/user/mo beyond, with Premium and Elite gated behind a sales call23.
  2. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “zero-effort visual documentation that protects teams and proves value.”47 “GPS-tagged, timestamped photos that are exactly what adjusters need during supplement meetings.” The founder on the Beam acquisition: “Beam was a natural fit because they’ve built the best AI estimating and payment tools for contractors, and that’s exactly the gap we wanted to fill.”8
  3. Strengths. Deep capital, a Series C at a $2 billion valuation, the first Nebraska startup to reach unicorn status, with roughly $68M ARR in 202426. Named roofing case studies at scale, including Apple Roofing (17 offices, 10 states) and Bone Dry Roofing (20 locations, 9 states), every one centered on photo documentation outcomes48. A founder with genuine roofing roots in the family business49.
  4. Weaknesses. Scope. The core product has no native CRM, no estimating, no scheduling, no dispatching, and no invoicing, and CompanyCam’s own blog promotes third-party integrations (Roofr for estimating, JobNimbus for CRM) as the answer to those gaps7. The Beam acquisition (March 2, 2026) brought estimating and invoicing under the same roof, but the press release states the companies “continue to operate independently,” so CompanyCam sells no native estimating or invoicing today8. Cost draws complaints: a G2 reviewer reported “almost $500/month just for photo management”50, and support is a recurring theme, including a CEO who waited over two months for a response on a single issue50.
  5. Recent dated activity. Series C from B Capital announced August 21, 2025, with unicorn status confirmed in November 202526. Twenty new integrations added through 2025, plus a mobile remodel and an Elite tier. Beam Finance acquired March 2, 20268.
  6. Overlap with PitchPro. Narrow today. CompanyCam overlaps only on photo documentation, which PitchPro can fold in as a feature tied to jobs. The CRM, estimating, and payment overlap exists in CompanyCam’s funding narrative and not in its shipped product.
  7. Inline whitespace. The entire quote-to-cash workflow sits open next to CompanyCam. A roofer using it still buys a CRM, an estimating tool, and a payment platform, then stitches them by hand, three or four subscriptions and manual handoffs7. CompanyCam even frames its photo work defensively, proving work was done for insurance and warranty rather than winning jobs47, which leaves “win more jobs” and “get paid faster” fully open for a platform that runs the whole pipeline in one seat.

2.4 EagleView, the measurement authority that is a supplier rather than a rival

URL. https://www.eagleview.com

Verbatim positioning. The homepage H1 is “Insight Is Power™” and the roofing page H1 is “Accurate Measurements. Confident Projects. Bigger Profits.”9 The entire value proposition is measurement-data delivery, with no mention of CRM, scheduling, payments, or business management anywhere on either page9.

  • The 7 Powers, real against claimed. EagleView holds a genuine cornered resource, the strongest of any company in this report. It owns 300+ patents covering aerial capture and measurement methods, listed in the site footer and actively enforced, with the Nearmap litigation filed in 2021 settling May 29, 202651. Behind the patents sits a 25-year imagery library, 3.5 billion-plus images covering 96% of the US population, that competitors cannot replicate quickly51. Insurance lock-in reinforces it, 19 of the top 20 insurers rely on EagleView9. The resource has caveats: coverage gaps force a Hover or manual fallback on newer and tree-obstructed properties45, and Nearmap continues operating after settlement51. The claimed network effects do not hold; its integrations are one-way data exports, not multi-sided networks52.
  1. Target and scope. Roofing and exterior contractors who need remote measurement, insurance restoration contractors, enterprise chains, plus insurance carriers, government, and solar9. EagleView One launched June 24, 2025, as a unified subscription platform, and Eagleview Horizon launched April 21, 2026, as an agentic GeoAI engine, both expansions inside the measurement and imagery lane rather than moves into CRM or payments53.
  2. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “1-Inch Accuracy.” “98%+ Accuracy on property measurements.” “19/20 - 19 of top 20 insurers rely on Eagleview.” “3 Billion+ Images captured nationwide.”9
  3. Strengths. The patent portfolio and imagery library above51, PE capital from Vista Equity and Clearlake for sustained investment27, the ServiceTitan integration (June 5, 2025) that flows measurements into a competitor platform52, and independent accuracy validation at 98.77%53.
  4. Weaknesses. Price is the dominant complaint category, with per-report fees that owners call “pricey to use regularly” and a January 2025 review where a basic report underestimated shingles by six squares and management refused to confirm whether the $87 premium report would have been correct54. Acquisition friction is real: the EagleView One page offers no self-serve pricing, no trial, and no sign-up, only a sales contact form, which disadvantages smaller contractors used to self-serve SaaS24.
  5. Recent dated activity. Independent 98.77% accuracy benchmark and ServiceTitan integration in June 20255253; EagleView One launched June 24, 202553; Eagleview Horizon launched April 21, 202653; Nearmap patent dispute settled May 29, 202651.
  6. Overlap with PitchPro. Minimal and complementary. EagleView sells measurement data; PitchPro runs the business around it. PitchPro can import aerial views from EagleView as a feature, so the framing is never EagleView-as-rival, it is PitchPro as the platform that makes EagleView data actionable.
  7. Inline whitespace. Everything around the measurement sits open. EagleView tracks no leads, schedules no crews, generates no full proposals, collects no payments, and runs no homeowner portal55. A platform that consumes EagleView data and then runs the lead, the proposal, the schedule, the invoice, and the payment owns the entire workflow EagleView does not touch.

3. Whitespace Map

Competitive positioning quadrant

  • PitchPro vs. named competitors Figure 2. Only Roofr shares PitchPro’s roofing-native all-in-one corner. Each dot is a competitor; dot size reflects market presence. The rust-shaded quadrant is the roofing-native all-in-one ground
  • contested only by Roofr.

An open lane only matters if PitchPro can hold it. Lanes a competitor has left genuinely open are PitchPro’s to take; lanes that look open carry a reason the incumbent leaves them, and that reason decides whether PitchPro can move in.

Operational backbone (clean two-way QuickBooks, real mobile field app), genuinely open. Roofr’s QuickBooks is Beta and one-directional11 and it has no native app10; roofers report sync errors and broken mobile across incumbents12. This is the most defensible lane and the one PitchPro should anchor to.

Predictable total cost of ownership for the active $2-10M roofer, genuinely open. Per-report fees and add-on stacks make incumbent bills swing with volume; an active Roofr roofer can reach roughly $519/mo before extra reports13. A flat bill for that buyer is uncontested.

Roofing-native quote-to-cash in one seat (against horizontal and photo-only tools), genuinely open. Housecall Pro ships no roofing workflow5 and CompanyCam ships no CRM, estimating, or payments7. The lane is open against those two; it is contested against Roofr.

  • Rural and field-signal coverage, apparently open. Roofr’s satellite gaps and browser-only access underserve rural and low-signal crews1039. It stays open because Roofr has chosen to ship a PWA later rather than a native app, and because satellite imagery economics favor dense suburban markets, so the lane is real but narrow.

Commercial and storm-restoration insurance workflow, apparently open but defended. Roofr targets small residential and cedes complex commercial35; AccuLynx owns the insurance-restoration workflow, the documented primary adoption trigger after a hail event56. This lane looks open next to Roofr, but a deep incumbent holds it, and PitchPro’s own insurance report is not carrier-approved, so this is not PitchPro’s lane to claim today.

Proprietary roofing pricing and profitability data, open as an opportunity, not a present asset. No competitor holds a roofing pricing and job-profitability dataset that compounds with volume. PitchPro could build one through its AI quote ingest, but it has zero customers and no flywheel yet, so this is a moat to earn rather than a lane to claim57.

4. Total Cost of Ownership Logic

Sticker price misleads in this category because the variable fees and add-ons decide the real bill. The figures below are individually sourced; there is no aggregate comparison table because the totals depend on a roofer’s report volume and add-on mix.

  1. Roofr’s variable cost is the measurement report. Roofr charges $13 per report on paid plans and $19 per report on the free tier, on top of the monthly subscription, with no included reports13. An active roofer pulling roughly 20 reports per month pays $260-$380 in report fees alone before subscription13. A Scale-plan roofer ($349) who also takes the Instant Estimator (about $125/mo) and Measure+ (about $95/mo) add-ons pays about $519 per month before a single report beyond the plan13.
  2. The flat-rate comparison cuts both ways. PitchPro’s flat $399 for up to 20 people, unlimited use, with no per-report fee, wins on a predictable bill for the active, growing roofer who pulls high report volume and needs add-ons13. A low-volume roofer tells a different story: roughly five reports per month on Roofr Essentials ($249) plus about $65 in reports comes to about $314, under $39913. The honest claim is predictability for the active $2-10M roofer, never cheapest across the board13.
  3. Incumbent stacks set the upper bound. A roofer assembling best-in-class point tools (CRM plus scheduling plus estimating plus photos plus measurement) spends $500-$1,200 per month across three to five platforms that do not natively integrate58. Per-user incumbents climb with headcount: AccuLynx Essential starts at $250/mo with per-user tiers above, and JobNimbus runs about $225/mo plus $25-$75 per user plus texting, so a ten-person team can top $1,200/mo59. EagleView adds $15-$38 per report on top, with no flat SaaS tier19. CompanyCam adds $79/mo for three users and $29 per user beyond23. Against those stacks the flat $399 is a real predictability advantage for a team that would otherwise pay per seat and per report.

5. Migration Friction and Anti-Switching Triggers

Switching costs differ by incumbent, and the triggers that make a roofer start looking are specific and repeatable.

  1. Roofr. Migration friction is moderate. Workflow capture (CRM, proposals, payments) creates data lock-in, and Roofr keeps account data for reactivation, though it confirms data export on cancellation17. The switching triggers are measurement reliability and the missing mobile and accounting plumbing. Listener cues a rep should catch: “the pitch came back wrong again,” “my last reports came back as unavailable imagery”39, “there’s no real app, my crew opens the website on their phones”10, and “QuickBooks won’t sync the way I need it to”11.
  2. Housecall Pro. Migration friction is moderate, anchored by QuickBooks sync and customer history on Essentials and above. The triggers are roofing-fit and billing friction. Listener cues: “it’s not really built for roofing”5, “everything’s another add-on”42, “the price crept up faster than I expected”60, and “I cancelled and they kept trying to charge me”43.
  3. CompanyCam. Migration friction is shallow because photos export cleanly. The triggers are scope and cost. Listener cues: “I’m paying almost $500 a month just for photos”50, “it has no real scheduling features”61, “support went dark on me for weeks”50, and “I wish it had a timeclock so I’d stop buying another app”62.
  4. EagleView. Migration friction is moderate for enterprise and insurance accounts built around its data formats, low for smaller contractors who trial Roofr or Hover. The triggers are price and turnaround. Listener cues: “the expedite fee feels like a scam, they hold the report to make me pay”63, “I sat on hold over an hour and nobody came on the line”64, “they charged me more to remove buildings I didn’t order”54, and “tree cover made me switch half my reports”45.

6. Compliance Posture

Roofing software has no single industry regulator the way trucking has FMCSA. The category’s compliance pressure comes from three operational areas: insurance-claim documentation standards, payment processing, and data ownership on exit. The market evidence here is sourced, and anything PitchPro has not confirmed is tagged for confirmation rather than asserted.

  1. Insurance and storm-restoration documentation. Storm-damage and insurance-restoration workflow is the primary adoption trigger for roofing software, and the practical standard is the ability to produce supplement documentation, adjuster coordination, and Xactimate-format estimates within the 48-hour window after a hail event56. AccuLynx’s market leadership is specifically attributed to this feature depth56. This is the operational table stake for any roofer doing insurance work.
  2. Payment processing. Roofr discloses its processing terms publicly, credit card at 2.8% plus $0.30 and ACH at 0.5%17, which sets the disclosure expectation a buyer will compare against. A live checkout that actually processes is the baseline; PitchPro’s checkout sitting in Stripe test mode fails it today.
  3. Data ownership and exit. Roofers treat clean data export on cancellation as a real decision factor, sharpened by complaints that an incumbent “hold their data hostage”65 and that a promised full file download on cancellation did not arrive66. A buyer evaluating PitchPro will ask what leaves with them if they go.

Auditable-specifics checklist.

Already shipped (confirm before claiming) Expected by the $2-10M ICP, still needed
Stripe payment integration present [client to confirm live status] Live, processing checkout (currently Stripe test mode)
AI quote / PDF-ingest feature claimed on site [client to confirm functioning demo] A working, demonstrable demo (currently a placeholder)
Flat-rate billing model [client to confirm contract and refund terms] Documented data-export-on-cancellation policy
Roofing-native CRM and proposal scope [client to confirm depth] Two-way QuickBooks sync with reconciliation
Aerial import from measurement providers [client to confirm which providers] Carrier-accepted insurance/supplement documentation (self-disclosed as not carrier-approved)

7. Vulnerability Watch

Each exposure below names the vulnerability, the verbatim artifact and its source, whether the window is open or closing, and a sales motion a rep can run head-to-head. The forensic catches come first because they are the cheapest to act on and the most dangerous to PitchPro itself.

7.1 PitchPro’s own self-inflicted exposures

  1. The Vulnerability. PitchPro’s site claims to be the “only all-in-one” roofing platform, a claim a single look at Roofr falsifies, and it ships a broken demo and a checkout in test mode, so the product cannot be shown or bought.
  2. The Evidence. Roofr’s homepage meta reads “All-in-one roofing software with CRM, instant estimates, proposals, measurements, payments & material ordering”1, and four of the top six roofing vendors use the identical “all-in-one” phrase33. PitchPro’s placeholder demo and Stripe test-mode checkout are first-party observations on its own live site.
  3. The Window. Open and fully under PitchPro’s control. It closes the moment PitchPro edits its own copy, replaces the demo, and takes Stripe live. PitchPro fixes this itself in an afternoon of editing and engineering.
  4. Recommended Response. Before any outreach, replace “the only all-in-one” with the defensible specific, “the roofing-native platform built to run itself for the $2-10M contractor,” take Stripe out of test mode, and ship a working demo. A rep should never walk a prospect to a placeholder; fix the surface first.

7.2 Roofr ships visible execution gaps on its own pages

  1. The Vulnerability. Roofr’s polish does not match its all-in-one claim: a live grammar error, asterisked support claims, and features still marked “Coming soon.”
  2. The Evidence. The pricing page reads verbatim “Roofr is the only roofing software that have a no-monthly cost option” [sic], the “Dedicated Account Manager” and “unlimited onboarding calls” claims carry an asterisk reading “Available for paid subscribers,” Change Orders are listed “Coming soon,” and QuickBooks is labeled “Beta,” all live as of June 202667.
  3. The Window. Closing slowly. Roofr can fix copy at any time, but the underlying feature gaps (Change Orders, two-way QuickBooks, native mobile) require shipped product, which its own roadmap defers into 202610.
  4. Recommended Response. When a prospect leans toward Roofr, ask what their bookkeeper does at month-end, then point out Roofr’s QuickBooks is Beta and one-way11, and ask how their crew works a job in a low-signal area, then note there is no native app10. Let the gaps surface against the prospect’s own workflow.

7.3 Housecall Pro’s all-in-one claim collides with its add-on bill

  1. The Vulnerability. Housecall Pro markets itself as the “#1 all-in-one solution” while charging separately for nearly every capability beyond the base plan, and its roofing fit is a landing page without roofing features.
  2. The Evidence. The proposal tool, service plans, pipeline, call answering, campaigns, websites, accounting, payroll, and GPS are all separate paid add-ons42, the roofing page carries no aerial measurement or insurance workflow5, and roofing is absent from the plan-recommendation quiz6.
  3. The Window. Open and durable. Closing it would require Housecall Pro to build roofing-specific product across 50+ trades, with no signal it intends to.
  4. Recommended Response. For a roofer comparing against Housecall Pro, walk through one storm job: aerial measurement, supplement documentation, shingle-by-square costing. Housecall Pro does none of it natively45. The horizontal tool asks a roofer to bend their workflow to a generic shape.

7.4 CompanyCam narrates a platform it has not built

  1. The Vulnerability. CompanyCam’s funding-round language reaches into CRM, sales, and payments, but the product is still a photo tool, creating a gap between narrative and capability.
  2. The Evidence. The Series C narrative names a vision to help contractors “do good work, win the right customers, and get paid fast”46, while the Beam acquisition press release states the companies “continue to operate independently,” so no native estimating or invoicing exists today8.
  3. The Window. Open now. It narrows as Beam integration matures, so the head-to-head advantage is strongest while CompanyCam remains a photo tool with an aspirational story.
  4. Recommended Response. When a prospect uses CompanyCam and likes it, agree it is the best photo tool, then ask what they use for CRM, estimating, and payments, and add up those subscriptions. PitchPro consolidates that stack and keeps photos tied to the job.

7.5 EagleView’s coverage and attribution leave a self-serve and reliability gap

  1. The Vulnerability. EagleView gates EagleView One behind a sales call, runs a coverage-gap fallback that frustrates smaller contractors, and shows an unnamed testimonial on a hero page.
  2. The Evidence. The EagleView One page offers only “Reach out to sales to get started,” with no self-serve pricing or trial24; a roofing-page testimonial is attributed only to “Contractor, Midwest Roofing Co.” with no name, beside fully named quotes68; and a reviewer switched about half their reports away over tree-obstruction failures45.
  3. The Window. Open for the self-serve smaller contractor. EagleView’s enterprise and insurance lock-in stays durable, so the gap sits at the small-contractor edge while the core holds firm.
  4. Recommended Response. Position PitchPro as the platform that consumes EagleView data rather than competes with it. For a smaller roofer who finds EagleView slow to buy and pricey per report, offer a flat platform that imports the measurement and runs everything around it.

8. Cliche Saturation Index

A phrase qualifies as saturated when three or more competitors use it, each occurrence sourced to a live page. PitchPro must retire or differentiate any saturated phrase it currently leans on.

Phrase Competitors using it Recommendation for PitchPro
“all-in-one” Roofr1, Housecall Pro14, plus AccuLynx and JobNimbus among the top six33 Must retire as a primary claim; PitchPro currently over-uses it (“only all-in-one”) and it is false
“built for roofers” / “by roofers” Roofr15, CompanyCam (founder roofing roots)49 Differentiate; true but no longer ownable on its own
“win more jobs” Roofr15, Housecall Pro5 Differentiate; pair with the runs-itself specific
“get paid faster” Housecall Pro5, Roofr (small-business page)15 Acceptable as supporting copy, not a headline
“save time” Housecall Pro5, Roofr15 Retire as a differentiator; it is table stakes
“streamline” CompanyCam16, Roofr15 Differentiate; replace with the concrete automation it describes
“AI-powered” Housecall Pro22, CompanyCam8, EagleView (Horizon)53 Differentiate; name the specific roofing job the AI does, not the buzzword

The one phrase PitchPro must retire outright is “all-in-one,” and especially “the only all-in-one,” because it is both saturated and falsified133.

9. Battle Cards

One card per competitor. Each carries a positioning summary, attack lines, defense lines (spoken responses only), proof points, a spoken script, and a case-math block. The defense lines and proof points each rest on a verifiable competitor fact.

9.1 Battle Card: Roofr

30-Second Positioning Summary. Roofr is the funded all-in-one that already owns the headline PitchPro wanted. It is real, it is broad, and it prices flat with a free tier. PitchPro does not beat it on breadth or price. PitchPro beats it on the operational backbone Roofr has not shipped: a real mobile field app and clean two-way QuickBooks, plus a predictable flat bill for the active roofer Roofr’s per-report fees punish.

Attack Lines.

  • “Roofr is browser-only, no native app yet. When your crew is on a roof in a dead-signal pocket, what do they open?”
  • “Roofr’s QuickBooks is still Beta and one-way. Ask your bookkeeper what that means at month-end.”
  • “At twenty reports a month plus the add-ons most growing roofers need, Roofr runs past five hundred a month before extras. Our bill is flat.”

Defense Lines (spoken responses).

  • When a prospect says Roofr is free to start, the rep answers that the free tier charges $19 a report with no monthly cover, so an active roofer’s report fees alone outrun a flat plan fast, and PitchPro’s price stays the same whether they pull five reports or fifty13.
  • When a prospect says Roofr is backed by ABC Supply and looks safer, the rep acknowledges the funding is real and then moves the ground to daily use, the missing native app and the Beta one-way QuickBooks that a roofer feels every week1011.
  • When a prospect says Roofr already does everything, the rep notes Change Orders are still “Coming soon” on Roofr’s own pricing page and production scheduling is a 2026 roadmap item, then asks whether the prospect needs those now6710.

Key Proof Points.

  • Roofr has no native iOS or Android app; the platform is browser-based, with a PWA on the roadmap that remained unshipped as of April 202610.
  • Roofr’s QuickBooks integration is Beta, one-directional, and Scale-plan only11.
  • Roofr charges $13 per report on paid plans and $19 on free, always on top of subscription13.

When the prospect asks about Roofr. “Roofr is the strongest option in this category, and I respect it. Where it costs you is the parts you live in every day. There’s no real mobile app, so your crews work off a browser in the field. The QuickBooks sync is still in Beta and only runs one way, so your books need hand-fixing. And the per-report fees climb with your volume. We’re flat at $399 for up to twenty people, the app works on the roof, and the accounting closes clean.”

Case math for this deal.

  • Vertical-matched proof point: [client to confirm] PitchPro is pre-revenue and has no published roofing outcome a rep can read aloud yet.
  • TCO worksheet (fill in live): Prospect’s reports per month: ____ x Roofr $13 = ____. Plus Roofr Scale $349 = ____. Plus add-ons they need (Instant Estimator ~$125, Measure+ ~$95, SMS $49): ____. Roofr monthly total: ____. PitchPro flat: $399 for up to 20 people, $0 per report. Compare.

9.2 Battle Card: Housecall Pro

30-Second Positioning Summary. Housecall Pro is a horizontal field-service tool that put up a roofing landing page without roofing features. It is strong for HVAC and plumbing and thin for roofing. PitchPro is built for roofing end to end. The contrast a roofer feels is the storm job: measurement, supplement, material costing, none of which Housecall Pro does natively.

Attack Lines.

  • “Housecall Pro doesn’t even list roofing in its own plan-recommendation quiz. Ask yourself why.”
  • “Their roofing page has no aerial measurement and no insurance workflow. That’s the heart of a storm job.”
  • “The ‘all-in-one’ price is the base plan. The proposal tool, pipeline, and call answering are each another line item.”

Defense Lines (spoken responses).

  • When a prospect already runs Housecall Pro for another trade, the rep grants it is a capable horizontal tool, then points out the roofing page (published October 2025) carries zero aerial measurement, insurance supplement, or storm-damage features, so roofing-specific work lives outside it5.
  • When a prospect cites Housecall Pro’s 200,000 Pros, the rep notes that scale is spread across 50+ trades with no roofing-specific depth, and that the roofing page reads the same as the cleaning and plumbing pages5.
  • When a prospect calls Housecall Pro all-in-one, the rep lists the separate paid add-ons (proposals, pipeline, call answering, campaigns, accounting, payroll) and asks what the real monthly total comes to once those are on42.

Key Proof Points.

  • Housecall Pro’s roofing page carries no aerial measurement, no insurance supplement workflow, and no storm-damage tracking5.
  • Roofing is absent from Housecall Pro’s plan-recommendation quiz, which lists ten other trades6.
  • Core capabilities (proposals, pipeline, call answering, campaigns, accounting, payroll, GPS) are separate paid add-ons beyond the base plan42.

When the prospect asks about Housecall Pro. “If you ran an HVAC shop I’d tell you to look hard at Housecall Pro. For roofing it’s a landing page, not a product. There’s no aerial measurement, no insurance supplement workflow, no storm-damage tracking, and roofing isn’t even in their own plan quiz. And the all-in-one price is just the base; proposals and pipeline and call answering are each extra. We’re built for roofing, and the price you see covers your team.”

Case math for this deal.

  • Vertical-matched proof point: [client to confirm] no published PitchPro roofing outcome available pre-revenue.
  • TCO worksheet (fill in live): Housecall Pro base plan for prospect’s team size: ____. Plus add-ons they need (proposal tool, pipeline, call answering, campaigns at separate rates): ____. Housecall Pro monthly total: ____. PitchPro flat: $399 for up to 20 people. Compare, and weigh the roofing features Housecall Pro cannot supply at any price.

9.3 Battle Card: CompanyCam

30-Second Positioning Summary. CompanyCam is the best photo tool in roofing and nothing more. A roofer using it still buys a CRM, an estimating tool, and a payment platform on top. PitchPro folds the photo job into a platform that runs the whole pipeline, so the prospect drops three subscriptions and the manual stitching between them.

Attack Lines.

  • “CompanyCam is excellent at photos and does no CRM, estimating, scheduling, or invoicing. What are you paying for those?”
  • “Their own blog tells you to add Roofr for estimates and JobNimbus for CRM. That’s three bills and three logins.”
  • “The Beam estimating story is a press release. The companies still run independently, so it isn’t in the product yet.”

Defense Lines (spoken responses).

  • When a prospect loves CompanyCam, the rep agrees it is best-in-class for photos, then asks which separate tools handle their CRM, estimating, and payments, since CompanyCam’s core product handles none of those7.
  • When a prospect points to the Beam acquisition as proof CompanyCam is going all-in-one, the rep notes the press release states the two companies “continue to operate independently,” so estimating and invoicing are not native today8.
  • When a prospect balks at switching photos, the rep notes CompanyCam photos export cleanly and PitchPro keeps photos tied to each job, so the documentation survives the move7.

Key Proof Points.

  • CompanyCam’s core product has no native CRM, lead pipeline, estimating, scheduling, dispatching, or invoicing7.
  • CompanyCam acquired Beam on March 2, 2026, and the press release states both companies “continue to operate independently”8.
  • CompanyCam pricing starts at $79/mo for three users with a three-user minimum and $29 per added user23.

When the prospect asks about CompanyCam. “CompanyCam is the best photo tool out there, full stop. The catch is it only does photos. Their own blog points you to other tools for CRM and estimates, and the Beam estimating piece they announced still runs as a separate company. So you’re paying for photos plus a CRM plus an estimator plus payments. We run all of that in one platform and keep the photos attached to the job.”

Case math for this deal.

  • Vertical-matched proof point: [client to confirm] no published PitchPro roofing outcome available pre-revenue.
  • TCO worksheet (fill in live): CompanyCam for prospect’s seat count (3-user minimum at $79, +$29/user): ____. Plus their CRM subscription: ____. Plus their estimating tool: ____. Plus their payment tool: ____. Stack monthly total: ____. PitchPro flat: $399 for up to 20 people, one platform. Compare.

9.4 Battle Card: EagleView

30-Second Positioning Summary. EagleView is the measurement authority and a supplier to the workflow. It holds a real cornered resource in patents and imagery, and it sells no business software. PitchPro consumes EagleView data and runs everything around it. The rep frames PitchPro as the platform that makes that measurement actionable, never as a replacement for it.

Attack Lines.

  • “EagleView is measurement data. It doesn’t track a lead, build a proposal, or collect a payment. We do all of that.”
  • “Their platform hides behind a sales call, no self-serve pricing, no trial. We’re flat and you can see the price.”
  • “Per-report fees and expedite charges add up. We import the measurement and don’t charge you per pull to run your business.”

Defense Lines (spoken responses).

  • When a prospect treats EagleView as a competitor, the rep reframes it as a data source PitchPro imports, since EagleView sells measurement and imagery and no CRM, scheduling, or payments9.
  • When a prospect cites EagleView’s accuracy and authority, the rep agrees the data is the industry standard and notes PitchPro is built to consume it, so the prospect keeps the data they trust and gains the platform around it9.
  • When a prospect mentions EagleView’s per-report cost, the rep notes the dominant complaint on EagleView’s own reviews is price, including expedite fees, and contrasts a flat platform fee with the report itself5463.

Key Proof Points.

  • EagleView sells measurement, imagery, and geospatial data, with no CRM, scheduling, payments, or invoicing on either its homepage or roofing page9.
  • EagleView One offers no self-serve pricing, trial, or sign-up, only a sales contact form24.
  • Price is the dominant complaint category across EagleView’s Capterra reviews, including expedite-fee resentment5463.

When the prospect asks about EagleView. “EagleView makes the best measurement data in the business, and we don’t compete with it, we import it. They don’t run your CRM, your proposals, your schedule, or your payments. You keep the measurement you trust and run the rest of your business in one place, at a flat price instead of a sales call and a per-report meter.”

Case math for this deal.

  • Vertical-matched proof point: [client to confirm] no published PitchPro roofing outcome available pre-revenue.
  • TCO worksheet (fill in live): Prospect’s EagleView reports per month x ($15-$38 each): ____. This is a measurement cost PitchPro does not replace; PitchPro imports the data. PitchPro flat platform fee: $399 for up to 20 people, which replaces the CRM, estimating, scheduling, and payment tools EagleView never provided. Compare the platform spend, not the report.

10. Strategic Implications

Each implication below states the call, anchors it in evidence, and ends in an action that reinforces a Brief recommendation.

PitchPro cannot win the all-in-one breadth argument, so it must retire the claim. Roofr self-describes as all-in-one in five live locations and four of the top six vendors use the phrase133. Replace “the only all-in-one” everywhere with “the roofing-native platform built to run itself for the $2-10M contractor,” and edit the site copy now.

The operational backbone is the one provable competitor gap, so PitchPro must anchor its brand there. Roofr has no native app and a Beta one-way QuickBooks1011, and roofers complain about exactly these failures across incumbents12. Pour pre-revenue engineering into clean two-way QuickBooks and a real mobile field experience, and ship those before flashy modules.

The runs-itself promise cannot be shown today, so PitchPro must make it demonstrable before it leads with it. The demo is a placeholder and there are no customers to prove the claim. Build a working demo of the 15-second quote and the auto-advancing whiteboard, and gate the runs-itself headline behind that demo.

Price predictability beats price level against Roofr, so PitchPro must lead on a predictable bill and never on cheapest. Roofr is already flat with a $0 tier, and a low-volume roofer can undercut $399413, while an active roofer’s per-report fees push past $51913. Position pricing as one predictable bill with no per-seat tax and no add-on surprises, and build a net-cost worksheet reps complete with each prospect’s real report volume.

The broken checkout blocks revenue, so PitchPro must repair its own surfaces before any outreach. The checkout sits in Stripe test mode and the about page is empty, both first-party. Take Stripe live, replace the demo, and fill the about page, then launch outreach.

Search demand in the category is real, so PitchPro must capture it rather than cede it. The category shows active paid-search and SEO competition with 7+ ranking roundups, while PitchPro’s own blog sits empty69. Fill the blog with roofing-specific content and run a small paid-search test on “roofing CRM” and “roofing software,” then let cost-per-activated-trial decide the budget split.

Consolidation is a copyable wedge that any funded rival can match, so PitchPro must name its one structural candidate as an opportunity and instrument for it. Roofr already replicated consolidation and holds the live structural moat through ABC Supply equity and the SRS feed320, while PitchPro has no proven moat57. Instrument every quote’s pricing and realized profitability from day one so a roofing pricing dataset can compound, and present that flywheel as a moat to earn, never as one already held.

EagleView is a complement, so PitchPro must frame it as a data source and never as a rival. EagleView sells measurement and imagery with no business software9 and integrates into PitchPro’s competitors as a supplier52. Build and market the aerial-import path as a feature, and position PitchPro as the platform that makes EagleView data actionable.

Sources

  • Roofr homepage and small-business landing page, live fetch June 2026; Roofr Series B press release, prnewswire.com 302362118, January 28 2025 (CMP-035).

  • Roofr Series B announcement, TCV-led with ABC Supply participating, roofr.com/blog and prnewswire.com 302362118, January 28 2025 (CMP-036).
  • Roofr pricing page, flat plans and permanent $0 Starter tier, roofr.com/pricing, live fetch June 2026 (CMP-038, CMP-045).
  • Housecall Pro roofing software page, housecallpro.com/industries/roofing-software/, live fetch June 19 2026; meta published October 17 2025 (CMP-024).
  • Housecall Pro pricing page plan-recommendation quiz, housecallpro.com/pricing/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-030).
  • CompanyCam scope assessment, fieldcamp.ai and roofingsoftwareguide.com reviews 2026; companycam.com integrations blog (CMP-005).
  • CompanyCam acquisition of Beam Finance, March 2 2026, siliconprairienews.com (CMP-002).
  • EagleView homepage and roofing-and-siding page, eagleview.com, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-013).
  • Roofr no native mobile app and roadmap items, roofr.com/pricing and roofingsoftwareguide.com review, April 2026 (CMP-042).
  • Roofr QuickBooks integration Beta and one-directional, roofingsoftwareguide.com review, April 2026 (CMP-043).
  • JobNimbus QuickBooks sync failures, Apple App Store reviews July 11 2025 and December 21 2023 (CUS-019, CUS-020).
  • Roofr per-report TCO analysis and PitchPro flat-rate comparison, myquoteiq.com/compare/roofr (Feb 2026), roofingsoftwareguide.com, contractortoolstack.com, capterra.com/p/208102/Roofr/reviews (CMP-047).
  • Housecall Pro sitewide positioning meta tag, housecallpro.com/pricing/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-023).
  • Roofr claimed differentiators, roofr.com homepage and product pages, live fetch June 2026 (Roofr competitor profile, roofr.com).
  • CompanyCam positioning, job-tracking page title tag and roofing industry page, companycam.com, indexed June 2026 (CompanyCam competitor profile).
  • Roofr full platform feature set, roofr.com and roofr.com/pricing, live fetch June 2026; Series B press release January 2025 (CMP-037).
  • Housecall Pro add-on structure including sales proposal tool, housecallpro.com/pricing/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-027).
  • EagleView per-report pricing $15-$38, roofingsoftwareguide.com/guides/eagleview-pricing/ (MKT-006).
  • Roofr SRS Distribution real-time pricing integration, prnewswire.com 302497413, July 7 2025; digitalcommerce360.com July 8 2025 (CMP-041).
  • Housecall Pro QuickBooks gated to Essentials and above, housecallpro.com/pricing/ and fieldcamp.ai review 2026 (CMP-025).
  • Housecall Pro pricing and plan add-ons, housecallpro.com/pricing/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-027, Housecall Pro profile).
  • CompanyCam pricing, $79/mo three-user minimum, $29/user, roofingsoftwareguide.com, fieldcamp.ai, scanmanifold.com 2026 (CMP-003).
  • EagleView One sales-gated acquisition, eagleview.com/eagleview-one/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-022).
  • Housecall Pro $125M Series D and footprint, housecallpro.com funding press release and CEO announcement; Crunchbase/Tracxn, June 2026 (CMP-029, CMP-033).
  • CompanyCam Series C and $2B valuation, prnewswire.com 302535365, August 21 2025; siliconprairienews.com November 2025; getlatka.com 2024 (CMP-004).
  • EagleView ownership, Vista Equity (2015) and Clearlake Capital (2018), prnewswire.com and eagleview.com blog (CMP-020).
  • Roofr review ratings, Capterra 4.6/101 and G2 4.9/40, capterra.com/p/208102/Roofr/reviews and Roofr profile (CMP-040, Roofr profile).
  • Housecall Pro G2 rating 4.3/204 against own 4.7 claim, g2.com/products/housecall-pro/reviews and site footer, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-032).
  • CompanyCam review ratings, G2 4.7/32 and Capterra 4.6, g2.com and capterra.com, 2026 (CMP-006).
  • EagleView Capterra rating 4.3/44, capterra.com/p/175004/EagleView-Roofing/reviews, fetched June 19 2026 (CMP-016).
  • Roofing software no-man’s-land gap for $2-10M roofers, toricentlabs.com, roofingcontractor.com, roofingsoftwareguide.com 2026 (MKT-008).
  • “All-in-one” saturation across roofing vendors, AccuLynx and JobNimbus homepages, roofingsoftwareguide.com, projul.com 2024-2026 (MKT-007).
  • Roofr process power assessment, satellite/Google imagery, capterra.com/p/208102/Roofr/reviews (Roofr profile).
  • Roofr SMB targeting and ceding of mid-market, roofr.com/more-info/roofing-software-for-small-businesses and roofr.com/pricing, live fetch June 2026 (CMP-046).
  • Roofr permanent free Starter tier, roofr.com/pricing and roofingsoftwareguide.com review, April 2026 (CMP-045).
  • Roofr named customer case studies, roofr.com homepage, live fetch June 2026 (Roofr profile).
  • Roofr measurement reliability complaint, Capterra, Christopher S., July 10 2022 (CMP-040, CUS-021).
  • Roofr imagery unavailability complaint, Capterra, Rich M., August 6 2021 (CUS-022).
  • Roofr pricing repriced to $19 free / $13 paid reports, roofr.com/product-blog/updates-to-roofrs-pricing, February 1 2025 (Roofr profile).
  • Roofr pricing tier restructure to Starter/Measure+/Essentials/Scale, roofingsoftwareguide.com and roofr.com/pricing, March 2026 (Roofr profile).
  • Housecall Pro add-ons as separate paid line items, housecallpro.com/pricing/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-027).
  • Housecall Pro BBB complaints, 76 over three years, billing/cancellation pattern, bbb.org Housecall Pro profile, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-026).
  • Housecall Pro CEO change to Stan Chia, housecallpro.com/resources/housecall-pro-names-new-ceo-stan-chia/, June 15 2026 (CMP-028).
  • Housecall Pro roofing-unfit assessment and EagleView tree-obstruction switching, myquoteiq.com 2026; Capterra EagleView, Hyrum S., May 28 2025 (CMP-034, CUS-045).
  • CompanyCam Series C vision language ahead of product, prnewswire.com 302535365, August 21 2025 (CMP-012).
  • CompanyCam mission statement, defensive documentation framing, businessmodelcanvastemplate.com and companycam.com/about (CMP-009).
  • CompanyCam named roofing case studies, companycam.com case studies for Apple Roofing, Bone Dry Roofing, Reliant Roofing, Best Roofing (CMP-008).
  • CompanyCam founder roofing roots, companycam.com founders story and frontlines.io podcast (CMP-007).
  • CompanyCam pricing and support complaints, G2 and Capterra reviewers, 2026 (CMP-006).
  • EagleView patent portfolio, imagery library, and Nearmap settlement May 29 2026, eagleview.com press release and site footer (CMP-014).
  • EagleView ServiceTitan integration, globenewswire.com, June 5 2025 (CMP-018).
  • EagleView One (June 24 2025) and Horizon (April 21 2026) launches and 98.77% accuracy, eagleview.com news and press releases (CMP-017).
  • EagleView shingle underestimate and refused comparison, Capterra, Deborah B., January 10 2025 (CMP-021, CUS-023).
  • EagleView adjacent whitespace, capabilities it does not provide, EagleView competitor profile (EagleView profile).
  • Storm-restoration insurance workflow as primary adoption trigger, roofingsoftwareguide.com, acculynx.com, knockbase.com (MKT-009).
  • PitchPro no proven moat; Roofr holds live structural moat, surviving arguments moat assessment (MKT-014, CMP-036, CMP-041).
  • Roofing point-solution stack cost $500-$1,200/mo across 3-5 platforms, roofingsoftwareguide.com and toricentlabs.com 2026 (MKT-004).
  • Per-user incumbent pricing, AccuLynx and JobNimbus, projul.com pricing analyses 2026 and roofingsoftwareguide.com (MKT-005).
  • Housecall Pro price escalation sentiment, contractortoolstack.com, 2026 (CUS-043).
  • CompanyCam no scheduling features complaint, Capterra, Ryan B., April 17 2025 (CUS-032).
  • CompanyCam timeclock and checklist feature request, Capterra, Brad H., May 17 2023 (CUS-031).
  • EagleView expedite-fee complaint, Capterra, Jose N., August 21 2019 (CMP-020, CUS-027).
  • EagleView customer-service hold-time complaint, Capterra, Phillip C., May 25 2023 (CUS-025).
  • AccuLynx data-hostage complaint, Capterra, Jay Z., June 10 2026 (CUS-009).
  • AccuLynx file-export-on-cancellation failure, G2, Eugene C. (CUS-011).
  • Roofr live-page forensic catches, grammar error, asterisked claims, Coming soon and Beta labels, roofr.com/pricing, live fetch June 2026 (CMP-039).
  • EagleView unnamed testimonial attribution, eagleview.com/industry/roofing-and-siding/, live fetch June 19 2026 (CMP-019).
  • Active paid-search and SEO competition in roofing software category, comradeweb.com, whatconverts.com, myquoteiq.com, localroofingseo.agency (MKT-015).
Fenn & Hart
